coredata.dc:title: The valorisation of grass waste for the green synthesis of graphene quantum dots for nonlinear optical applications
coredata.dc:creator: Danial, Wan Hazman
Abdullah, Mundzir
Abu Bakar, Mohamad Aizat
Yunos, Muhammad Syafiee
Ibrahim, Abdul Razak
Iqbal, Anwar
Adnan, Nurul Nadia
coredata.dc:description: The recent years have seen an increase in efforts to develop a simple and less complex method of converting waste materials into graphene nanomaterials. Herein, we present the valorisation of grass waste for the green synthesis of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) using hydrothermal technique. The efficacy of using three different precursors that had been sourced from green waste materials to produce GQDs was investigated; namely, grass waste, and grass waste-derived cellulose, and cellulose nanocrystals. The resulting GQDs were characterised using UV–Vis spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, Raman spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), atomic force microscopy (AFM), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The UV–Vis analysis revealed the existence of π-π* transition of the CC bond of the GQDs samples while the FTIR results showed the samples contained typical functional groups; such as O–H, CO, and C–O. The surface functionalisation of the GQDs was further corroborated by the XPS analysis. The Raman analysis indicated that the structure of the GQDs was highly functionalised, with a more prominent D-band than G-band indicating a high ID/IG ratio. The morphological analyses, which were conducted using TEM and AFM, showed that the particles of the samples were uniform in size and circular in shape. The AFM analysis found that the thickness of the GQDs samples ranged between 0.5 and 2.6 nm, implying that the samples contained 1–3 layers of GQDs. Further analysis using the Z-scan technique indicated that the GQDs exhibited strong nonlinear refraction and nonlinear absorption which holds great promise for nonlinear integrated applications such as optical limiting, optical switching, image transmission, logic devices and mode-locked laser system, and so on.

The setup of z-scan measurement technique.
(a) Dried grass waste, (b) SEM image of cellulose, and (c) TEM image of CNCs prepared from the grass waste.
The schematic of GQDs formation from the three precursors.
Optical images of GQDs dispersed in water. (a) GQD-CNCs, (b) GQD-CELL and c) GQD-GW under the normal daylight exposure, and (d) GQD-CNCs, e) GQD-CELL and f) GQD-GW under the UV exposure.
The UV–Vis, fluorescence excitation (λex = 410 nm) and emission (λem = 490 nm) spectra of (a) GQD-CNCs, (b) GQD-CELL and (c) GQD-GW.
FTIR spectrum of (a) GQD-CNCs, (b) GQD-CELL and (c) GQD-GW.

The close and open aperture z-scan profile of GQD-CNCs (a and b), GQD-CELL (c and d), and GQD-GW (e and f).
Data of ID/IG ratio for GQDs samples.
Nonlinear optical parameters of GQD-CNCs, GQD-CELL and GQD-GW.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The recent years have seen an increase in efforts to develop a simple and less complex method of converting waste materials into graphene nanomaterials. Herein, we present the valorisation of grass waste for the green synthesis of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) using hydrothermal technique. The efficacy of using three different precursors that had been sourced from green waste materials to produce GQDs was investigated; namely, grass waste, and grass waste-derived cellulose, and cellulose nanocrystals. The resulting GQDs were characterised using UV–Vis spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, Raman spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), atomic force microscopy (AFM), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The UV–Vis analysis revealed the existence of π-π* transition of the CC bond of the GQDs samples while the FTIR results showed the samples contained typical functional groups; such as O–H, CO, and C–O. The surface functionalisation of the GQDs was further corroborated by the XPS analysis. The Raman analysis indicated that the structure of the GQDs was highly functionalised, with a more prominent D-band than G-band indicating a high ID/IG ratio. The morphological analyses, which were conducted using TEM and AFM, showed that the particles of the samples were uniform in size and circular in shape. The AFM analysis found that the thickness of the GQDs samples ranged between 0.5 and 2.6 nm, implying that the samples contained 1–3 layers of GQDs. Further analysis using the Z-scan technique indicated that the GQDs exhibited strong nonlinear refraction and nonlinear absorption which holds great promise for nonlinear integrated applications such as optical limiting, optical switching, image transmission, logic devices and mode-locked laser system, and so on.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors confirm that the data supporting the findings of this study are available within the article

ce:section.ce:para: In recent times, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) is one of the most widely known carbon-based material that have gained much attention by researchers throughout the globe due to its unique and exceptional properties. The properties of the GQDs include excellent water solubility, nanoscale size, good chemical stability and photoluminescence, and these have enabled them to be used in various promising applications included but not limited to biosensors, solar cells, electronic devices, drug delivery systems and catalysts to name a few [1–4]. The emergence of GQDs applications especially in nanotechnology has urge the scientist to explore and synthesise GQDs using rapid, simple and eco-friendly procedure to avoid toxic by-product that can be harmful to the environment [5].

Despite many researchers have proposed various routes for the synthesis of GQDs, the processes typically involve the use of toxic reagents, acidic or alkaline conditions, expensive precursors or carbon source, tedious and required multistep to obtain the final product. Besides, it is still a considerable challenge to develop more facile and green approaches for the GQDs synthesis using abundant and inexpensive precursors [6]. Therefore, a green synthesis GQDs using low-cost precursors and environmentally friendly approach should be considered as a top-notch endeavour. In general, GQD synthesis is classified as either a top-down or bottom-up approach [7,8]. The top-down approach involves the breaking of large structures of sp2 carbon into small molecules via various exfoliation techniques [9,10] while the bottom-up method involves the formation of small organic molecules in the GQDs via chemical reactions [11]. There are several attempts have been made for the green synthesis of GQDs by employing inexpensive and natural materials as carbon precursors; such as nutmeg seeds [12], lignocellulosic residues [13], pistachio shells [14], sugarcane bagasse [15] and Mangifera indica (mango leaves) [16].

On top of that, the scientific community recognised that in order to reach the sustainable development goals (SDGs), it is essentially important to manage the abundant of biomass waste that might be useful for the synthesis of graphene-based materials. Cellulose-based biomass is one of the most abundantly produced biomass waste at both agricultural and domestic levels [17,18], of which grass waste is one of the most prolifically produced. Globally, areas with substantial green agriculture consistently produce vast quantities of grass waste. Grass waste also constitutes a major portion of the solid waste in green waste. As green waste is constantly produced, it requires considerable storage space. This makes the management of green waste rather complicated and expensive. Although composting is an alternative method of green waste management, the presence of recalcitrant components; such as lignin and cellulose; significantly increases the composting time and impedes the process. Furthermore, if grass and green waste are not transported to landfills, they are either left to accumulate in an unsightly heap or burnt, which certainly harms the environment. Therefore, an avenue to transform the waste material into valuable nanomaterial would be significant so as to achieve the sustainable green synthesis goals. Owing to this regard, this work addressed the idea and effort to valorise the abundant grass waste materials for the green synthesis of GQDs and the exploration of its potential use in nonlinear optical field. The quantum confinement effects in quantum dot materials could induce superior nonlinear optical response, essential in broadening the use of quantum dots in a variety of fascinating fields outside of linear optics, including ultracompact nonlinear optical components, multiphoton imaging, and nonlinear optical signal processing.

Graphene-based material is no stranger in optical nonlinear studies [19]. Although a monolayer of graphene is known to have remarkable optical properties in the broadband region where it is highly transparent with only 2.3% absorbance, combining multiple monolayers of graphene produces a highly absorbent material. The unique electronic structure of graphene is admirable especially in the field of nonlinear optics (NLO) [20–22] where the despite having the centre of inversion symmetry, it can still give rise to the third-order susceptibilities. In the realm of photonics, the drive toward integrated devices represent a significant turning point that will lead to a wide range of new applications such as all-optical signal processing; ultra-low power all-optical switching; and quantum photonics. Photonic devices benefit from a variety of factors that result in enhanced field-strength and increased interaction length, which reduce the power levels required for nonlinear effects, in addition to the traditional benefits of integration, such as reduced system size and dramatically lower cost per element.

Several techniques have been employed to study the nonlinear properties of material such as z-scan technique [23], four wave mixing [24], optical pump-intense THz probe [25] and optical spectroscopy system [26]. The z-scan approach among others, has the advantage of obtaining information on the magnitude of real and imaginary parts of nonlinear susceptibilities, as well as their sign. Sheik Bahae developed the z-scan technique in 1989, which could be utilized for both solid and liquid samples [27]. Since then, it has become a crucial approach in the field of NLO because it capable of concurrently assess the magnitude of both nonlinear absorption (NLA) represented by nonlinear refractive index, n2 and nonlinear refraction (NLR) represented by nonlinear absorption coefficient, β. Previous studies on the NLO properties of graphene shows a huge NLO performance but the results vary based on dimension/structure of the graphene and measurement conditions. For instance, thin film graphene (2D form) synthesised from CVD method observed under ultrashort pulsed laser z-scan possess n2 in infrared regime of 10−8 cm2/W [28]. Graphene in reduced form under picosecond irradiation recorded n2 range of 10−12 cm2/W [29]. Graphene also have been incorporated into composite form with epoxy resin which produce a nonlinear refractive value of 10−8 cm2/W [30]. Further modification on graphene including the preparation of graphene in QDs form proves to have some improvement thanks to the strong quantum confinement effect, thus significantly modifies electron distribution due to the reduced dimension of the crystal to nanometre scale. Furthermore, the structure and size dependent property of QDs implies the tunability of the NLO response. In practice, however, shrinking the size does not always improve its NLO response. As a result, the measurement of the NLO parameters must be correlated with other information about the dimension and structure of the QDs to shine light on their unique structure-related properties.

This present study synthesised GQDs using a hydrothermal method and three different precursors derived from grass waste materials as the carbon source. The grass waste primarily comprised cellulose-based materials that can be subjected to several processes to produce cellulose and cellulose nanomaterials. The use of grass waste-based precursors; grass waste, cellulose, and cellulose nanocrystals (CNCs); were investigated in the production of GQDs. At the time of writing and to the best of our knowledge, the green synthesis of GQDs from grass waste has not been reported and examining the use of precursors is the most efficient method of utilising grass waste biomass. The schematic provided in Fig. 1 depicts the formation of GQDs from grass waste precursors. These GQDs were then characterised using ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectroscopy, Fluorescence spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, Raman spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), atomic force microscopy (AFM), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), whereas the NLO response was measured via single beam z-scan technique.

Grass waste was sourced from lawn mowing activities in Kuantan, Pahang, Malaysia. Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and sodium chlorite (NaClO2) were purchased from R&M Chemicals while hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and sulfuric acid (H2SO4) were purchased from Merck & Co., Inc. All chemicals were used as received, without any pre-treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of grass waste-derived cellulose and cellulose nanocrystals (CNCs)
ce:section.ce:para: The cellulose and CNCs were prepared and extracted from the grass waste using the procedures outlined by previous studies [31,32]. In brief, pre-treatment, alkali treatment, and bleaching were used to extract the cellulose from the grass waste. The pre-treatment process entailed boiling and grinding the grass waste using a mechanical grinder to produce smaller fibres. The alkaline treatment involved a reaction with 4% NaOH solution while a reaction mixture consisting of 1.3% NaOH (m/v), 5% H2O2 (v/v), and 0.7% NaClO2 (w/v) was used during the bleaching treatment. The cellulose fibres that formed as a result of these treatments were then subjected to acid hydrolysis using 64% H2SO4 to produce the CNCs. The hydrolysis process was terminated by quenching the cellulose fibres with 10-fold distilled water. The suspension was then centrifuged and dialysed against distilled water to remove excess acid until a constant pH was attained. A ZEISS® scanning electron microscope and a Hitachi® HT7700 transmission electron microscope were used to observe the morphology of the grass waste-derived cellulose and grass waste-derived CNCs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Green synthesis of graphene quantum dots (GQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: A hydrothermal method was used to green synthesise the GQDs from the grass waste, grass waste-derived cellulose, and grass waste-derived CNCs. Briefly, the collected grass waste was boiled in distilled water, filtered, ground using mechanical grinder, and dried in an oven at 80 °C for 90 min. Then, 50 mg of the dried grass waste was dispersed in 60 ml of distilled water before the mixture was sonicated for 30 min. The dispersion was then transferred into a 100 ml Teflon Autoclave flask and heated in an oven for 8 h at 200 °C. It was allowed to cool at room temperature for 10 min before the dispersion was centrifuged for 30 min at 9000 rpm/min to remove any undissolved particles. The supernatant was then filtered using 0.22 μm pore size PVDF membrane (Bioflow) to remove any large residue. This process was repeated using grass waste-derived cellulose and grass waste-derived CNCs precursors for the GQD synthesis. Throughout the rest of this study, the grass waste GQDs will be referred to as “GQD-GW”, the grass waste-derived cellulose GQDs will be referred to as “GQD-CELL”, and the grass waste-derived CNCs GQDs will be referred to as “GQD-CNCs".

A PerkinElmer® UV–Vis spectrometer was used to obtain the UV–vis spectra of the GQDs suspension produced. The fluorescence spectra of the GQDs were recorded with a LS 55 fluorescence spectrometer (PerkinElmer) using a quartz cuvette cell (path length: 1 cm). A PerkinElmer® FTIR spectroscope was then used to conduct an FTIR analysis using the attenuated total reflection (ATR) technique. The FTIR spectra between 4000 cm−1 to 600 cm−1 of the dried GQDs were obtained to identify the functional groups and chemical characteristics of the GQDs. A Renishaw® Raman spectroscope was used to analyse the structural configuration and purity of the GQDs while a ZEISS® Libra 120 TEM was used to characterise the morphology and size distribution of the GQD suspensions. A JEOL® JEM-ARM200F field-emission electron microscope was operated at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV to record high resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images. The morphology of the GQDs was also investigated using a Bruker® AFM. Lastly, a Kratos® AXIS Ultra spectrometer, equipped with an Al Kα X-ray source (1486.6 eV) at 10 mA for wide scan and 15 mA for narrow scan, was used to conduct X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) to analyse the surface chemistry and chemical bonding environments of the GQD samples.

To calculate the third order optical nonlinearity χ(3) of the samples, both real, Re χ(3) and imaginary parts, Im χ(3) are derived from the value of n2 and β which are obtained from the open and close aperture profile, respectively. Fig. 2 depicts the setup that was used for this purpose.

The Z-scan experiments were performed using a diode laser on continuous mode at an output wavelength of 637 nm as the light source. The beam was then focused using a −25 cm plano-convex lens. The waist (wo) of the laser beam when focused was 63 μm and the irradiance (Iₒ) reached the maximum value of 1.4 kW/cm2. The GQDs suspension was placed inside a cuvette with a light travel path length of 1 mm. This was then translated along the beam propagation from near field to far field. At the same time, the optical signals emitted by the moving samples were split using a 50:50 beam splitter. This was to enable both the open aperture; which consist of a focusing lens; and the closed aperture; with a mechanical iris as the blocking component; to capture the light. Variations in the captured light signals were then observed and measured using a digital oscilloscope for detailed analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of grass waste-derived cellulose and cellulose nanocrystals (CNCs)
ce:section.ce:para: The cellulose was prepared using several chemical treatments that rely on the use of grass waste (Fig. 3 a) as the precursor. These treatments were conducted to remove any inorganic or unwanted components without affecting the semi-crystalline cellulose [33]. As the amorphous region of cellulose is vulnerable to hydrolysis [34], subjecting the grass waste-derived cellulose sample to further acid hydrolysis produced the CNCs. Various factors; such as the concentration, temperature, and reaction time; of the hydrolysis treatment may influence the physicochemical characteristics of the CNCs [35]. Fig. 3 depicts the digital, SEM, and TEM images of the dried grass waste, grass waste-derived cellulose, and grass waste-derived CNCs samples, respectively. The SEM image seen in Fig. 3b provides the morphology of the cellulose fibres while the TEM images seen in Fig. 3c reveal the typical needle-like or whiskers-like structures of the CNCs. The diameter and length of the CNCs prepared ranged between 5 to 15 nm and 100–500 nm, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Green synthesis of graphene quantum dots (GQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Grass waste or grass clippings are mainly composed of carbohydrates including cellulose, and has never been used for the synthesis of graphene quantum dots (GQDs). Fig. 4 depicts the schematic of GQDs formation from grass waste, grass waste-derived cellulose, and grass waste-derived CNCs as precursors. The investigation on these precursors for the green synthesis of GQDs seems to be the most efficient ways to utilize the grass waste biomass. The mechanism of the hydrothermal reaction consisted of two main steps. The first step involved the hydrolysation of cellulose followed by the ring-closure condensation reaction of the resulting glucose. The presence of distilled water during the hydrothermal reaction yielded a high amount of H+ and OH− ions at high temperatures and pressures. The presence of H+ ions initiated the hydrolysation of the cellulose, which produced many smaller components; such as glucose and cellobiose. As the reaction progresses, the amount of glucose increased. This caused it to become the main product for the reaction. The hydroxyl group present on the glucose then reacted with the hydrogen atoms of the other glucose molecules while the formyl group of the glucose reacted with the hydroxyl group of the other glucose molecules [36]. As a result, the carbon atoms of the remaining glucose molecules formed covalent bonds with one another, which resulted in the formation of aromatic rings or ring closure condensation reactions. Fig. 4 illustrates a schematic of the GQD formation using the three precursors while Fig. 5 provides the optical images of the GQD products dispersed in water as observed under normal light and post-exposure to UV irradiation at 365 nm. An indicator of successful GQD production is the observance of a strong bluish fluorescence under UV exposure. However, as seen in the image, the GQD-GW, and GQD-CELL samples were not as fluorescent as GQD-CNCs. This may due to the quantum confinement effect or a size-dependent effect [37] from the use of different precursors.

Fig. 6 depicts the UV–vis spectra of the GQDs. The UV–vis analysis indicated that the maximum UV absorption of the GQD-CNCs, GQD-CELL and GQD-GW samples were approximately 236 nm, 250 nm, and 217 nm, respectively. These peaks may be attributed to the presence of the π-π* transition in the CC bonds, which forms the backbone of the GQDs [38–41]. However, the shift in the spectrum of the GQD-CNCs, GQD-CELL and GQD-GW was hypochromic compared to that of the π-π* transition in the CC bonds of graphene, which is about 268 nm [42]. This peak may have shifted to a shorter wavelength due to decreased π-conjugation in the GQDs structure as less energy is required for the transition [43]. Furthermore, the absorption peaks of the for GQD-CELL and GQD-GW samples shifted to longer and shorter wavelengths, respectively, compared to the GQD-CNCs sample. This indicated that the use of different precursors; i.e., grass waste, grass waste-derived cellulose and grass waste-derived CNCs; to synthesise GQDs may increase or reduce the π-conjugation network of the GQDs. The shift may also be due to the presence of auxochrome as a result of oxygen functionalisation in the GQD structure. Although graphene ideally consists of pure sp2 carbon bonds, graphene realistically varies in size, thickness and surface defects [44]. In the case of graphene quantum dots, the surface and edge defects (oxygen functionalisation) are rather prominent and in comparatively akin to graphene oxide nature. Therefore, despite the term “graphene quantum dots”, the synthesised GQDs, strictly speaking, can be also considered as graphene oxide quantum dots [45]. The fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of the GQDs samples are depicted in Fig. 6 inset. When excited at 410 nm, the GQDs samples revealed fluorescence properties and exhibited a fluorescence emission peak at 490 nm. The emission spectra of the GQDs had a broad peak ranged from 450 to 550 nm. The broad fluorescence emission spectral range follows the GQDs size distribution curve towards the higher wavelength [46]. Albeit the fluorescence mechanism is not completely understood, it can be expected that quantum confinement effect, surface defects, and edge structures have the main influence on the fluorescence feature [36].

An FTIR analysis was conducted to determine the presence of functional groups or chemical moieties in the GQD structure. Fig. 7 provides the FTIR spectra of the GQD samples. As seen, the spectra of all the GQD samples were relatively similar, with the exception that several peaks were lower in the GQD-CELL sample and less apparent in the GQD-GW sample than in the GQD-CNCs sample. The broad peak observed around 3340 cm−1 indicated the presence of the hydroxyl functional group or OH stretching vibration in the structure of the GQDs. The peaks seen at 2921 cm−1 and 2851 cm−1 may be attributed to the presence of symmetric CH2 and asymmetric CH2, respectively. However, fewer symmetric and asymmetric peaks were observed in the GQD-CELL and GQD-GW samples. This implied that hydrocarbon conversion occurred, thereby, rendering the formation of graphitic structures in the GQD-CELL and GQD-GW samples [46]. This indicates that the size of the precursor may influence the formation of aromatic rings or ring closure condensation reactions. The peaks observed around 1700 cm−1 may be ascribed to the presence of CO while the peaks at 1623 cm−1 may be attributed to the stretching of CC groups. The peaks seen at around 1424 cm−1 may also be attributed to the presence of CC stretching [47] while the strong peaks at 1034 cm−1 may be ascribed to C–OH stretching or in plane C–H bending [46,47]. Therefore, the presence of the CC peak implies that the GQDs are made of graphitic structures while the CO, O–H and C–O or C–H peaks indicate that the GQDs have moieties of CO, O–H, and C–O, or C–H.

Fig. 8 shows the TEM images of the GQD-CNCs, GQD-CELL and GQD-GW samples and the size distribution of the GQDs, which were measured with respect to the diameter of the particles. ImageJ was used to measure the spacing of the lattice fringes by calculating the spacing of the inverse fast Fourier transform (FFT) images. As seen in the TEM images, the GQDs were mostly uniform in size with circular or round particles that were similar to the findings of multiple studies on GQDs synthesis [46,48]. The average GQD size in the GQD-GW, GQD-CELL, and GQD-CNCs samples were 56.5 ± 9.91 nm, 6.06 ± 1.18 nm, and 4.38 ± 0.74 nm, respectively. The particle size of the GQD-GW sample was significantly larger than that of the GQD-CELL and GQD-CNC samples. The lattice fringes seen on the HR-TEM images indicate that the as-synthesised GQDs were crystalline. The lattice spacing, or the D-spacing, was also measured using the HR-TEM images and found to be 0.25 nm for both the GQD-CELL and GQD-CNC samples and 0.26 nm for the GQD-GW sample. These values very exceptionally close to the 0.246 nm GQD d-spacing value reported by an extant study [49].

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to analyse the surface chemistry and gather information on the chemical bonding environments of the GQD samples. As seen in Fig. 9 , the XPS survey spectra of all GQDs samples exhibited two main peaks representing C 1s at 284.67 eV and O 1s at 531.47 eV. Despite the detection of residual elements due to the alkaline treatments, bleaching, and acid hydrolysis process that were used to prepare the cellulose and CNCs, the survey spectra indicated that the GQD samples mainly consist of carbon and oxygen. However, the GQD samples contained slightly different ratios of C 1s and O 1s due to the different precursors used to green synthesise the GQDs (Fig. 9). This revealed the presence of oxygen-containing functionalities in the GQD structure as evident by the results of the FTIR analysis, which were also corroborated by the XPS spectra. The deconvolution and high resolution XPS spectra of the C 1s peaks of the GQD-CNCs, GQD-CELL and GQD-GW samples are shown in Fig. 9b, e, and 9h, respectively. The main peak at 284.6 eV can be attributed to the sp2 graphitic structure while the peaks at 286 eV, 287 eV, and 288 eV may be ascribed to C–O, CO, and O–CO, respectively. This revealed that the carbon atoms had functionalised with oxygen and possessed different chemical binding states. The high resolution XPS spectra of the O 1s peaks of the GQD-CNCs, GQD-CELL and GQD-GW samples are shown in Fig. 9c, f, and 9i, respectively. The O1s spectrum exhibits three main peaks at 531.8, 532.8 and 536 eV associated with CO, C–OH and adsorbed O, respectively [18,50]. Although the functionalisation of the carbon atoms was apparent in the high resolution XPS spectra, it was more evident in the spectra of the GQD-CELL and GQD-GW samples. This indicated that the use of different precursors had an effect.

An AFM analysis was conducted to investigate the morphology of the GQDs samples. Fig. 10 shows the AFM images of the GQDs and the thickness of the GQDs particles, which were analysed to approximate the graphene layers. The AFM analysis revealed that the thickness of the GQD-CNCs, GQD-CELL and GQD-GW ranged between 0.5 and 2.1 nm, 0.5–2.6 nm, and 0.5–2.3 nm, respectively. As such, the GQD particles may consists of one to three layers, which is comparable and similar to the findings of previous studies that involved GQD production [36]. As most of the prepared GQDs contained oxygen and hydrogen groups in their chemical structures, a thickness of multiple atomic layers may be expected.

The graphitic structure of the GQDs was investigated using Raman spectroscopy at a 785 nm excitation wavelength. As seen in Fig. 11 , the Raman spectra of the GQD samples showed two significant peaks at about 1506 cm−1 and 1338 cm−1. These were attributed to the well-ordered graphite or sp2 hybridisation (G-band) and the sp3 hybridisation (D-band), respectively [51]. The G-band in the spectrum demonstrates the amount of graphitisation on the GQDs structure and the D-band; which is the sp3 hybridisation; indicates the surface defects or functionalisation of the GQD structure. As seen in the Raman spectrums, the D-band were higher than the G-band. Table 1 shows the calculation of the ID/IG ratio for the Raman spectrum of the GQDs. The results showed that the GQD-CNCs sample had the highest ID/IG ratio followed by the GQD-CELL and GQD-GW samples. This indicates that sp3 hybridisation was higher in the GQD-CNCs sample than the GQD-GW and GQD-CELL samples. This is evident in the FTIR spectra as the peaks attributed to symmetric CH2 and asymmetric CH2 were accentuated in the FTIR spectrum of the GQD-CNC sample (Fig. 7). The higher D-band in the GQD spectrum may also reflect defects associated with grain boundaries and amorphous carbons [52]. Apart from that, the smaller size of GQDs (as observed in the TEM results) results in high edge detection under the Raman beam, which explains the higher ID/IG ratio of the GQD-CNC sample.

Under the laser irradiation, the samples showed variation in optical responses which are detected by the photodiodes in both open and close aperture arms. The data collected are then being analysed for their best fitting according to the z-scan equation employing Levenberg-Marquardt algorithm. Fig. 12 shows the open and close aperture z-scan profile of all GQDs samples obtained during samples translation in z direction. All GQDs exhibit similar trends of NLO responses during scanning process. As the samples moves along the z-axis parallel to the direction of the beam exposed the samples under variation in laser intensities from weak intensities in the closest and the farthest position of the stage and gradually reach the highest intensity at the middle position where the focusing point is. As expected, the samples give rise to a dominant pattern in the transmitted signals for both close and open aperture measurement. In close aperture, the movement of the sample towards the middle position increase the laser intensity exposure on the samples affecting the refractive index changes thus revealing the self-defocusing behaviour of the samples. This led to an increase in the intensity captured by the close aperture photodiode which forms a significant peak in the graph. The reverse of the observed trend is shown as the samples are moved away from the focusing distance towards the far field of the setup. The intensity captured fall sharply just right after it leaves the focusing point position before it returns to the similar initial intensity forming a significant valley next to the peak formation as shown in Fig. 12 a, c and e. The overall observed phenomena is well expected in accordance with the Kerr effect based on the light intensity-dependent refractive index where the refractive index of material changes under electric field influence, hence causing the trend of intensity variation where them laser beam are guided to a longer focusing path due to the material behaviour under exposure of high intensity irradiation.

Simultaneously in open aperture measurement, the sample movement along the z-axis produced signal that is captured in slightly different way by using focusing lens to capture every incident light signal into the photodiode. This enables the observation of the samples response where the intensity decreases as the samples get closer to the focal length position from a normal intensity at both ends of the translation stage. The valley in the transmitted light signals (refer Fig. 12 b, d and f) confirms the presence of nonlinear absorption in the samples under the exposed laser intensity. The valley formation is attributed to the reverse saturable absorption (RSA) that took place under high intensity irradiation on the samples where the samples are no longer behave as transparent as under low intensity irradiation. The RSA effect is shown by all three samples, and can be attributed towards the multiphoton absorption, involving simultaneous absorption of two or more photons to excite electrons to its excited electronic state.

To obtain the magnitude of n
2 , the close aperture transmittance profile along the z direction is normalized and fitted by using thermal lensing model based on Sheikh Bahae formalism [53]. The transmission value of close aperture is divided over the value of open aperture transmission to eliminate the nonparametric influence on the native n
2 value.

The nonlinear refraction observed from the three samples exhibit a negative nonlinear refraction which manifested from a self-defocusing behaviour of sample under continuous mode laser irradiation. This self-defocusing property could be a result of the sample's thermal nonlinearity, which develops as a result of the heat emitted as a result of absorbing laser light. Subsequently, the sample develops a temperature-dependent refractive index and acts like a thermal lens. Among those samples, GQD-GW possess the highest nonlinear refraction value at −10.89 × 10−16 cm2/W followed by GQD-CNCs and GQD-CELL measured at similar excitation intensity at 1.4 kW/cm2. This trend is in agreement with the intensity of the UV–vis and fluorescence spectra of the samples in Fig. 6. We can expect that the production of GQD-CELL possessed lower yield and concentration of GQDs compared to the other two. Therefore, by comparing the three samples, magnitude of n2 magnitude is concentration-dependent despite, in our study, the actual concentration of GQDs in the samples is not measured. This implies that when concentration rises, more molecules will be present to interact with the laser. As a result, more molecules are thermally agitated, which raises the thermal nonlinearity. Additionally, as laser power rises, the sample's thermal nonlinearity becomes more pronounced. The overall range of n
2 obtained from all samples is several time higher than reported by using other techniques [54]. It is also worth noting that the numbers of layers of the graphene might not affect the nonlinear refractive index value. The low photon energy of the light source used in this research however, in high intensity, did forced the exciton to absorb the incoming photon energy ignoring the Pauli's blocking via non-degenerate two-photon absorption.

The largest β observed comes from the GQD-CNC at 1.85 × 10−3 cm/W which is expected due to its smallest GQDs size followed by GQD-CELL and GQD-GGW. The QDs are known for its high surface-area-to-volume ratio which only get higher as the dimension become smaller. This is where the quantum confinement effect took place between the layers of GQDs. The NLO properties observed via both open and close aperture are coming into crescendo with further calculation of the overall χ (3) . The χ (3) value reported in this research are in the range of 10−5 esu which is comparable to the value reported by the commercially manufactured graphene [55] and several times magnitude larger than others synthesised graphene [56,57] and bulk dielectrics [58]. The excellent performances in both NLR and NLA together with their green synthesis route from the synthesised GQDs are much preferred in diverse NLO applications such as all-optical signal processing; ultra-low power all-optical switching; quantum photonics; imaging and microscopy and many more.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were successfully synthesised from three different precursors; grass waste, grass waste-derived cellulose, and grass waste-derived cellulose nanocrystals (CNCs); using a hydrothermal method and distilled water as the sole reactant. The UV–Vis analysis of the GQDs showed a significant peak, which indicates the presence of the π-π* transition of the CC bond in the GQDs structure. The FTIR analysis of the GQD revealed the presence of peaks corresponding to the presence of O–H, CC, and C–O in the GQD structure. The XPS analysis also alluded to the presence of oxygen-containing functionalities in the GQDs. The Raman spectrum revealed higher D-band than G-band, indicating that the structure of the GQDs was highly functionalised. The TEM images showed that the average size of the GQDs were 4.38 ± 0.74 nm, 6.06 ± 1.18 nm, and 56.5 ± 9.91 nm for GQD-CNCs, GQD-CELL, and GQD-GW, respectively. The thickness of the GQDs were about 0.5–2.5 nm, indicating that they are one to three-layers of GQDs. Nonlinear optical response of the synthesised GQDs samples based on z-scan technique reveals dominant nonlinear absorption coefficient, β compared to its nonlinear refraction response which is in good agreement with other reported works. These findings offer a viable foundation for integrated chip-scale nonlinear optical systems.
